_description: Model derivations for Norway vs England QF pricing (2026-07-11). RULE1 restored this session (Smarkets fetched FIRST, before any estimate was finalized) after yesterday's Spain-Belgium postmortem found the model-only experiment fetched market data too late to act on the one question where it mattered most. Every model output below is shown next to its market cross-check; gaps over ~15-20pp are flagged and resolved by blending, not left in a file nobody reads until after the match.

elo.note: Moderate favorite, not a blowout mismatch like Spain(+216.61)/Belgium -- relevant to the SYMMETRIC_REGIME_COVERAGE check below.

poisson_goal_rates.note: This lambda_total is LOW relative to both teams' own 2026 scoring environments (England's 5 games averaged 3.2 total goals; Norway's averaged 4.2) and relative to the market-implied lambda from the O/U2.5 goals price (2.999, back-solved via brentq). Flagged and corrected in the goals-dependent questions below (Q4, Q6) rather than trusted raw -- likely because the global Elo-Poisson coefficients are calibrated on a large low-scoring-skewed historical panel (49,400 matches) that doesn't fully capture these two specific teams' unusually open 2026 form.

market_cross_check.note: Model and market agree closely on the win/draw/loss split -- the relative-strength read is sound, only the total-goals VOLUME dimension (below) is off.

monte_carlo_simulation.note: Independent Poisson goal counts, goal times sampled uniform(0,93). The uniform-time assumption doesn't capture the real-world second-half goal bias (fatigue, subs, chasing scorelines) -- see Q13 resolution, where this sim output is corrected against both a 60-match corpus base rate and the market's equivalent 'highest scoring half' price.

market_gap_log._description: Every case this session where |model - market| exceeded ~15pp, what was checked, and how it was resolved. This log is the direct fix for the Spain-Belgium Q12 failure mode (market flag seen, not acted on).

entries.question: 3+ total goals
entries.recheck: Both teams' own 2026 scoring logs run far hotter than the model's total lambda (England 3.2 goals/game, Norway 4.2 goals/game, vs model's 2.254 combined). Market-implied lambda (2.999) matches the empirical read far better than the model's own lambda (2.254).
entries.resolution: Blended toward market + empirical scoring logs, away from the raw Poisson output. Final: 0.54.

entries.question: Both teams to score
entries.recheck: Norway scored AND conceded in literally all 5 of its games this tournament (5/5 BTTS). England BTTS in 3/5. Same root cause as the goals-total gap above -- the model's low total-goals lambda understates both teams' actual attacking output.
entries.resolution: Blended heavily toward market + Norway's 5/5 empirical BTTS record. Final: 0.58.

entries.question: Jude Bellingham 2+ SOT
entries.recheck: StatsBomb historical panel (2022 WC, n=5, his only prior major tournament): 2+ SOT in 0/5 games, ceiling was 1 SOT. The 2026 empirical rate is driven almost entirely by his last 2 games (3, 3 SOT) after 3 games at 2, 0, 1 -- a short hot streak, not a form change confirmed by any independent source. Market and multi-year historical record agree against the recent streak.
entries.resolution: Shaded down sharply from the empirical 2026 rate toward market, this is the single largest correction this session and the clearest example of the recheck discipline working as intended. Final: 0.32.

entries.question: Harry Kane 2+ SOT
entries.recheck: Smaller gap than Bellingham's (under the 15pp trigger), included here for contrast: market already sits between the historical caution (0.167) and the 2026 hot form (0.60), i.e. the market has plausibly already done this same blending. No further correction needed beyond a small nudge.
entries.resolution: Set close to market with a slight upward nudge for genuine recent shot-volume increase. Final: 0.50.

entries.question: 24+ total shots
entries.recheck: No exact market line exists (Smarkets offers 25.5, not 23.5) -- rather than discard the market entirely as NA (last session's default), backed out an implied combined-shots lambda from the O/U25.5 price via brentq and re-evaluated at the 24+ threshold. This is a new technique this session: use near-miss market lines to calibrate the model's distribution shape instead of only using exact 1:1 matches.
entries.resolution: Blended model and market-calibrated figure. Final: 0.68.

entries.question: Norway 4+ corners
entries.recheck: Same near-miss-market-calibration technique (Smarkets offers Norway O/U4.5, not 3.5). Small gap, both well below the raw 2026 empirical rate (0.80, n=5, too thin to trust alone).
entries.resolution: Blended model and market-calibrated figure. Final: 0.63.

symmetric_regime_coverage_check._description: Explicit check this session, per the Spain-Belgium POSTMORTEM.md finding that this check was applied to only one side of a paired count-prop (Spain's own SOT, not Belgium's). Norway vs England has an equivalent paired prop: England 6+ corners / Norway 4+ corners.
symmetric_regime_coverage_check.elo_diff_context: England favored by only 97.97 Elo points, a MODERATE edge -- not the 216.61-point blowout-level gap that drove Spain's 67.9% possession suppression of Belgium yesterday. Dixon-Coles gives a genuinely competitive 45/29/26 win/draw/loss split, not a lopsided one.
symmetric_regime_coverage_check.england_corners_check: Is Norway a weak defensive/low-possession opponent that would inflate England's corner count relative to England's average? Norway's possession share varies widely by opponent quality (43-66%) and Norway has shown they can sit compact against stronger technical sides (66.4% possession conceded to Brazil in the R16 upset, suggesting a well-organized low block, not an easy walkover). No basis found for shading England's corner prop up beyond what EB shrinkage already reflects.
symmetric_regime_coverage_check.norway_corners_check: Is England a much stronger attacking side than Norway's other opponents, that would suppress Norway's OWN corner count (the exact mechanism that hurt Belgium's SOT prop yesterday)? England is a stronger opponent on paper (Elo +97.97) but this is a 98-point gap, not a 217-point gap, and England's own defensive record this tournament is not dominant (conceded in 3/5 games, including 2 against Mexico in a chaotic R16). No basis found for shading Norway's corner prop down beyond the EB shrinkage + market-calibration already applied.
symmetric_regime_coverage_check.conclusion: Checked both directions explicitly, as required. Neither prop needs additional asymmetric shading beyond the EB-shrinkage and market-calibration already applied above -- the underlying reason (this matchup isn't a blowout-level mismatch, unlike Spain-Belgium) is different from a false-negative miss; it's a genuine absence of the risk factor that applied yesterday.

cards_model.note: Excellent model/market agreement on penalty-or-red (0.3844 vs 0.3836) -- no correction needed, strong validation of the component-wise EB-shrinkage approach.

compound_corners_and_shots.note: No market exists for this exact compound question. Computed via Skellam distribution on each marginal (corners: shrunk lambdas 5.77 vs 4.45; shots: raw 2026 means 16.0 vs 10.6), then combined assuming partial (not full) positive correlation between the two territorial-dominance indicators, since a match where England dominates one dimension plausibly but not certainly dominates the other too.

haaland_goalscorer_model.2026_empirical_rate: 4/4 games played (1 DNP), i.e. scored in literally every game he featured in this tournament, 7 goals total
haaland_goalscorer_model.note: Same underlying tension as the Yamal case in Spain-Belgium but in the OPPOSITE direction -- there, a hyped player was underproducing relative to market price; here, a genuinely red-hot tournament top-scorer has a market price that already looks like it's pricing in a very live opponent-defense discount for facing England specifically. Norway's own prior match (Brazil vs Norway) independently validated 'bump Haaland above market on knockout-stakes regime-coverage reasoning' at 0.55 vs market 0.48, and it hit. Not chasing the unsustainable 100% empirical rate (small n=4, no team can price a striker at 90%+ under RULE6 discipline anyway), but leaning above market again given the validated precedent and continued elite production. Final: 0.48.
